levels.grammarNote: Arabic nouns are either masculine (مذكر, mudhakkar) or feminine (مؤنث, mu'annath). Most masculine nouns have no special ending. Add ة to get the feminine form: طالب → طالبة.
The ة (ta marbuta) ending is the clearest marker of a feminine noun. It is pronounced as '-a' in isolation and '-at' when followed by another word in a genitive construction.
Adjectives also have masculine and feminine forms. The adjective must agree with the noun it describes: بيت كبير (a big house, masc.), غرفة كبيرة (a big room, fem.).

levels.front: bread (masculine noun — adjective must be masc.)
levels.grammarNote: خبز (bread) is masculine, so its adjectives use the masculine form: خبز طازج (fresh bread). قهوة (coffee) is feminine: قهوة طازجة (fresh coffee).

levels.front: coffee (feminine noun — adjective must be fem.)

levels.grammarNote: When describing yourself, the adjective must match your gender: أنا سعيد (male), أنا سعيدة (female). The same applies to all predicative adjectives in equational sentences.
Some nouns are feminine without the ة ending — usually because of natural gender or convention. أم (umm = mother), بنت (bint = girl), شمس (shams = sun), أرض (ard = earth). These must be memorized.
وجبة (meal) ends in ة, so it's feminine. Its adjective لذيذ becomes لذيذة. Always check the noun's gender before choosing the adjective form.
